title: Preparation and ionic conductivities of the plasticized polymer electrolytes based on poly(methyl methacrylate-co-alkali metal methacrylate)
abstract:
Preparation and Ionic Conductivities of the Plasticized 
Polymer Electrolytes Based on Poly( methyl 
methacrylate-co-alkali metal methacrylate) 
CHUL-HWAN KIM,' HEE-TAK KIM,' JUNG-KI PARK,',* SUNG-IN MOON; and MOON-SU YOON' 
'Department of Chemical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, 373-1, Kusung-dong, 
Yusung-gu, Daejon, 305-701, Korea; 'Korea Electrotechnology Research Institute, P.O. Box 20, Changwon, Korea 
SYNOPSIS 
Ionic conductivities of the polymer electrolytes prepared from the ionomer (poly(methy1 
methacrylate-co-alkali metal methacrylate)), lithium perchlorate, and ethylene carbonate 
as a plasticizer, were studied as a function of the ion content and the alkali-metal cation 
of the ionomer. It was possible to obtain tough films with room-temperature ionic conduc- 
tivities of - 
S/cm. The maximum ion conductivities of the polymer electrolytes were 
obtained at the ion content of 5 mol % for both Li and Na ionomer. The effects of the ion 
content of the ionomer on the ionic conductivities of the polymer electrolytes were mainly 
interpreted in terms of the characteristics of the ion aggregate formed in the polymer 
electrolytes. The thermal dependence of the ionic conductivity was shown to be a non- 
VTF pattern in some of the polymer electrolytes investigated, which is expected to be due 
to the presence of the ion aggregate. 0 
1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
Keywords: ionomer polymer electrolyte ion aggregate ionic conductivity ion content 
INTRODUCTION 
Solid polymer electrolytes with high ionic conduc- 
tivity have been of interest in theoretical and basic 
research as well as for the development of novel 
device such as high energy density batteries. These 
electrolyte systems consist of a solid polymer host 
in which a salt, usually an alkali metal salt, has 
been dissolved. Matrix polymers for such electro- 
lytes include polyether,' poly(ethylene-imine),2 and 
poly(alky1ene ~ulfide).~ 
Combinations of these ma- 
trices with LiC104 show ionic conductivities of 
10-6-10-9 S/cm at 25"C, which is poor for appli- 
cation. 
Because of the detrimental effect of the crystals 
of the polymers on ionic conductivity, a number of 
approaches have been taken over the years to sup- 
press the crystallization process. The use of the in- 
herently amorphous polymers such as poly(propy1ene 
oxide): the irradiation treatment to induce crosslik- 
* To whom correspondence should be addressed. 
Journal of Polymer Science: Part B: Polymer Physics, Vol. 34,2709-2714 (1996) 
0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
CCC 0~7-6266/96/162709-06 
ing,5 and the incorporation of  plasticizer^^'^ are 
amongst the alternatives which have been employed. 
Recently, solid polymer electrolytes with high ionic 
conductivity in the neighborhood of 
S/cm at 
ambient temperature have been reported.8-1° These 
electrolytes were obtained by immobilizing solutions 
of Li salts in organic polymer matrices. The typical 
examples of such electrolytes were those based on 
polyacrylonitrile (PAN), poly(viny1 chloride) (PVC), 
and PMMA, etc. In the present study, we report a 
new class of polymer electrolyte which includes the 
ionomer, poly(methy1 methacrylate-co-alkali metal 
methacrylate) (MMA-co-MMA). 
Ionomers have been the subject of attention to 
produce a single ion cond~ctor,'~-'~ 
in which only 
one of the positively and negatively charged ions 
can be mobile under the influence of electric field, 
but they show very low ionic conductivities for the 
operation of solid-state batteries. However, studies 
of ionomers for bi-ionic conductor, in which the low 
molecular salt is incorporated and both of the cation 
and anion contribute to ionic conduction, has not 
been yet reported. In this study, we synthesized the 
ionomer, MMA-co-MMA and prepared the polymer 
2709 
 2710 
KIM ET AL. 
electrolytes by dissolving the ionomer in an appro- 
priate ethylene carbonate (EC)/LiClO, solution. For 
both the lithium and the sodium carboxylate io- 
nomer, the effects of ion content and the tempera- 
ture dependence of the ionic conductivity of the 
polymer electrolytes were investigated. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
Preparation of MMA-co-MAA and Its lonomer 
The MMA-MAA copolymers were prepared by so- 
lution copolymerization of MMA and MAA using 
AIBN as an initiator. To the reactor fitted with a 
mechanical stirrer, condenser, nitrogen inlet and 
outlet, the mixed monomer, and solvent (tetrahy- 
drofuran) were added, and then the initiator (AIBN) 
was injected. The mixture was heated to 6OoC with 
stirring for 24 h under nitrogen atmosphere. After 
the polymerization was completed, the product so- 
lution was precipitated in cold methanol, filtered, 
washed two times in methanol, and dried under vac- 
uum at 150°C. The functionality of the precipitated 
polymer was determined by the titration with 0.1 N 
NaOH methanol solution using phenolphthalein as 
an indicator. Neutralization of the acid groups into 
Li and Na salts was accomplished in THF/methanol 
(90/10, v/v) solutions to which calculated amounts 
of standard solutions of the respective hydroxide in 
methanol were added. The neutralized copolymers 
were then recovered by solvent casting in a hood 
and then dried at 180°C under vacuum. 
Preparation of Polymer Electrolytes 
The appropriate amount of the ionomer, ethylene 
carbonate, LiClO,, and methanol as a cosolvent were 
well mixed and heated to about 80°C until the io- 
nomer and LiC104 were completely dissolved in the 
cosolvent. The viscous solutions were then placed 
on Teflon plates and preserved in a dried nitrogen 
atmosphere. After evaporation of the cosolvent, 
polymer composite electrolyte films were peeled from 
the plate. The thickness of the film was 25 -t 5 pm. 
The composition of the ionomer electrolytes was 
22.2/7.8/70 (ionorner/LiClO,/EC) by wt %. 
DSC Thermal Analysis 
Thermal behavior of the ionomers and their elec- 
trolyte films was studied by means of differential 
scanning calorimetry with heating rate of the 10°C/ 
min within an appropriate temperature range. 
FTIR Measurement 
The specimen for FTIR measurement was cast onto 
the KRS-5 window. The FTIR spectra were recorded 
in transmission on the Bomem-450 with the reso- 
lution of 4 cm-l in the vibrational frequency range 
of 400-4000 cm-'. 
Conductivity Measurement 
Ion conductivities of the films were measured by AC 
impedance method using a Solartron 1255 frequency 
response analyzer (FRA) coupled to an IBM PS/2 
computer. All measurements were carried out under 
dry nitrogen atmosphere, and stainless steel was 
used as blocking electrodes in both sides. All the 
samples were allowed to be equilibrated on the ex- 
perimental temperature in the air bath. The ionic 
conductivity was determined from complex imped- 
ance diagram in the frequency range of lo2-lo6 Hz. 
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION 
By comparing the FTIR spectra for the unneutral- 
ized copolymer of methyl methacrylate and meth- 
acrylic acid, and the corresponding alkali-metal io- 
nomers, it is possible to ascertain the desired chem- 
ical structure of the ionomer. In Figure 1, the 
expanded FTIR spectra for the unneutralized co- 
polymer and its alkali-metal ionomers are shown. 
For the ionomers new peaks for the carbonyl 
stretching band in the region of 1550-1650 cm-' did 
appear, although they could not be observed for the 
unneutralized copolymer. The frequencies of these

decrease of the force constant with increasing the
size of the cation. The strong peak centered at 1740
cm-' corresponds to the C = 0 stretching of the es-
The DSC thermograms for the unneutralized co-
polymer and the corresponding the Li and Na io-
nomer (ion content: 5 mol %) are represented in
Figure 2. The Li ionomer shows the higher glass
transition temperature than the Na ionomer. This
might be due to the difference in the charge density
of the cation of the ionic group in the ionomer. In
ionomer, the ionic groups are aggregated to form
multiplet, which acts as a physical crosslink and re-
duces the mobility of the chain near the multiplet.
Due to the higher charge density of the smaller cat-
ion, the ion-ion interaction between cation and
polymeric anion becomes stronger with decreasing
the size of the cation. Since the multiplet is formed
by this interaction, the stronger ion-ion interaction
results in the tighter multiplet, which means that
the ionic groups in the multiplet are more tightly
bound with each other. Since the tighter multiplet
binds the chains more effectively, the glass transition
temperature increases with decreasing the size of
Figure 3 shows the ionic conductivities of the
polymer electrolytes based on the Li ionomer as a
function of the ion content of the Li ionomer. The
polymer and its alkali-metal ionomers.
DSC thermograms for the unneutralized co-
Figure 3. Ion conductivities of the Li ionomer electro-
lytes as a function of ion content at various temperatures.
initial increase of the ionic conductivity with the
ion content could be due to the two factors which
are closely related to the mobility and the number
of the charge carrier. The first factor is originated
from the increase in the compatibility between the
ionomer and the plasticizer with increasing the ion
content of the ionomer. Due to the strong ion-dipole
interaction between the ionic groups of the ionomer
and the polar group of EC, the compatibility between
the ionomer and EC would be increased with the
increase of the ion content, which would result in
the generation of larger free volume in the polymer
electrolytes, and consequently the increase of the
mobility of the charge carrier. The second factor for
the initial increase is associated with the increment
of the charge carrier concentration by the increase
of the ion content. Since the lithium cation from
the ionomer can also contribute to the ionic con-
duction as well as the charge carriers from lithium
perchloroate, the total concentration of the charge
carrier should increase with the increase of the ion
content. However, the first factor, i.e., the increase
in the compatibility between the ionomer and the
plasticizer, might mainly attribute to the initial in-
crease, because the increased amount of the lithium
cation in the ionomer is quite small compared to
that of the added salt, which provides major charge
carrier in the polymer electrolytes. The relative
amount of the charge carrier increased with the in-
crease of the ion content from 3 to 5 mol % to that
of the incorporated LiC104 is 0.079, which is too
small to be a significant factor for the 10-fold in-
crease of the ionic conductivity.
With the increase of ion content above 5 mol 5%
in the ionomer, the ionic conductivity is shown to
decrease until the ion content has reached 7 mol %.
We expect that the decrease of the ionic conductivity
is associated with the formation of the ion aggregate
within the polymer electrolytes. The evidence of the
existence of the ion aggregate can be obtained from
the X-ray scattering data. The plots of X-ray scat-
tering profile for the polymer electrolytes with various
ion content of the ionomer were given in Figure 4.
From the X-ray scattering profiles, the peak corre-
sponding to the formation of the ion aggregate was
more clearly found at the scattering angle 20 = 0.9"
(scattering vector q = 0.032 k')
of 7 rnol % than for the other ion content. Though
it is not clearly understood at the present stage, the
ion aggregate seems to exist in the polymer electrolyte
near the ion content of 7 mol %. This fact suggests
that the ion aggregate attributes to the abrupt de-
crease of the ionic conductivity at 7 mol %. The ion
aggregate within the polymer electrolytes may reduce
the free volume of the matrix because the ion aggre-
gate can act as a physical crosslinking site and pro-
foundly reduce the mobility of the chain near the
aggregate. Therefore, the mobility of the charge car-
rier also can be significantly decreased, which would
be responsible for the abrupt decrease in the ionic
conductivity at the ion content of 7 mol %.
Above the ion content of 7 mol %, the ionic con-
ductivity was found to increase with the ion content
of the ionomer until the ion content reaches 10 mol
%. This might be caused by the loosening of the ion
aggregates in the polymer electrolytes. The mobility
of the charge carrier must be significantly improved
in the polymer electrolytes upon the destruction of
the ion aggregates, since the ion aggregate is sup-
posed to hinder the transport of the charge carrier.
The almost disappearance of the peak in X-ray
scattering profiles for the polymer electrolytes with
10 mol % ion content of the ionomer suggests that
the ionic units of the ionomer are homogeneously
dispersed in the matrix rather than forming the ion
It should be noted that these polymer electrolytes
are quite tough and the maximum ionic conductivity
found at ion content of 5 mol % quite exceeds
S/cm, which implies that these polymer electrolytes
can be one of the potential candidates for commer-
From the temperature dependence of ionic con-
ductivities for the polymer electrolytes prepared
from the various ionomers, it is possible to consider
the mechanism of the ionic conduction. Most poly-
mer electrolytes, including solvent-free polymer
Figure 4. X-ray scattering profiles for the polymer
electrolytes with the various ion contents of the Li io-
electrolytes as well as plasticized polymer electro-
lytes, have been reported to fit the two simple mod-
els, which describe the ionic motion in polymer elec-
trolytes. One is the Arrhenius form, and the other
is the VTF type." The simplest Arrhenius form is
known to be appropriate for the thermally activated
ionic motion whereas the VTF type has been suc-
cessfully applied to model the temperature depen-
dence of ionic conductivity for a variety of amor-
phous polymeric electrolytes based on the polymer
network and polymer gel, etc. For the plasticized
polymer electrolyte, it has been reported that poly-
mer electrolytes based on PMMA could be well de-
relation was given for the polymer electrolytes pre-
pared by the PVdF in the Arrhenius c00rdinate.l~
Figure 5 shows the Arrhenius plot for the elec-
trolytes consisting of Li ionomer, EC, and salt. The
temperature dependencies of ionic conductivity for
the polymer electrolytes based on the 3 and 10 mol
5% ionomer are shown to be concave down, which
implies that the ion conduction mechanism might
be described by the VTF model as conventional
polymer electrolytes have been. It is notable that
the other ionomer electrolytes with ion content of
5 and 7 mol % display the concave up pattern in the
Arrhenius plot of ionic conductivities. These con-
ductivity behaviors mean that the mechanism of ion
conduction in the temperature range considered
cannot be described by both the Arrhenius and the
VTF model. This might be driven by the ion aggre-
gate formed in the polymer electrolytes. From Figure
5, it is observed that the rate of increase in the ionic
IONIC CONDUCTIVITIES OF POLYMER ELECTROLYTES
Arrhenius plot for the Li ionomer electrolytes
conductivity with temperature for the 5 and 7 mol
% polymer electrolytes is sluggish at lower temper-
atures, and becomes higher in the range of higher
temperature. As the temperature increases, the ion
aggregate is expected to be loosened. In the forma-
tion of the ion aggregate in this system, the chains
of the ionomer are anchored at the ion aggregates,
which act as a physical crosslink. Since the mobility
of the ionomer chain is severely reduced by the an-
choring effect of the ion aggregates, the conforma-
tional entropy of the chain is greatly decreased. With
increasing the temperature, the driving force to in-
crease the entropy of the chain would increase and
lead to the loosening of the ion aggregate. The charge
Arrhenius plot for the Na ionomer electrolytes
trolytes with various ion contents.
DSC thermograms for the Li ionomer elec-
carrier encaged in the ion aggregate could be par-
ticipated in the transportation at higher temperature
due to the loosening of the ion aggregate. Since the
charge carrier bound at the relatively immobile re-
gion, the ion aggregate, is more difficult to be trans-
ported than the free charge carrier, the higher ac-
tivation energy in the Arrhenius plot could be found
in the higher temperature range.
The ion conductivities for the polymer electro-
lytes prepared with the Na ionomer are plotted on
the Arrhenius fashion in Figure 6. The temperature
dependence of the polymer electrolytes with the ion
content of 3 and 5 mol % showed patterns similar
to those for the Li ionomer electrolytes. For the 7
and 10 mol %, the discrete increase in the ion con-
ductivities with the increase of the temperature was
trolytes with various ion contents.
DSC thermograms for the Na ionomer elec-
below and above the melting transition found in the
DSC thermograms (Fig. 9) are compared, as shown
in Fig. 9(c and d), it was found that the intensity at
20 = 19", at which the strong peak was revealed for
EC as shown in Fig. 9(a), was greatly diminished
above the melting point, which indicates that the
melting endotherm of the polymer electrolytes was
corresponding to the fusion of the EC.
This work has been carried out with financial support of

Figure 9. X-ray diffraction data for: (a) ethylene car-
bonate, (b) Na ionomer (ion content of 7 mol %), (c) poly-
mer electrolytes based on the Na ionomer (ion content of
7 mol %) recorded at the temperature below the melting
transition, (d) polymer electrolytes based on the Na io-
nomer (ion content of 7 mol %) recorded at the temper-
ature above the melting transition.
observed, which could be explained with the thermal
analysis of the polymer electrolytes. Figures 7 and
8 display the DSC thermograms for the polymer
electrolytes prepared from the Li and the Na io-
nomer with various ion contents, respectively. The
thermal analysis for the polymer electrolytes pre-
pared from the Na ionomer reveals the formation
of EC crystal around 25'C, whereas that for the
polymer electrolytes from the Li ionomer does not
show the significant melting endotherms. We might
conclude that the difference in the thermal depen-
dence of the ionic conductivity between the Li and
the Na ionomer electrolytes is caused by the for-
mation of EC crystal in the polymer electrolyte based
on the Na ionomer because the EC is the only com-
ponent capable of crystallization. When the EC
crystalline phase exists in the polymer electrolytes,
the motion of charge carrier would be hindered and
the number of charge carrier also would be de-
creased. For that reason, the ionic conductivities of
the ionomer electrolytes must be greatly enhanced
upon the melting transition (25°C). Our expectation
was supported by the X-ray diffraction data (see Fig.
9) for pure EC, the pure Na ionomer, and the poly-
mer electrolytes prepared from the Na ionomer.

polymer electrolyte recorded at the two temperatures
